speaker: interviewee
text: [SIDE CONVERSATION]

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we continue with your telling about the transport, is there anything else during your time specifically in Budapest? Any experiences that you wanted to be sure to mention?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were-- there were many things that were going on. It's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sort of hard to believe, maybe, for you, and sometimes even for me, that the period of three months of March 19 through June 30, 1944 was probably more heavily loaded with events and impact on-- on my life than any other-- not three months, but any other three year period. The-- there were many things that were going on and in which I was involved. One was rescue activities. Deportations in the Hungarian countryside started, I believe, on May 10. Maybe the 9th or 11th, I don't know. That's when the deportations started.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean? What that meant was that as Hungary was occupied on March 19, and some parts of Hungary weren't occupied until March 20 and 21, very soon thereafter came not only the decree about the Jewish star, but also the ghettoization decrees throughout the country, except Budapest. Budapest was not ghettoized, except as I mentioned before, in June.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so-- and very soon, on-- as I say, around May 10, the deportations started. And of course we have numerous refugees coming from the countryside, those who were not caught, who came to Budapest and had to be looked after. There had to be people helped to come from the countryside to Budapest. There was an intense trade in false papers. All of us who, you know, had-- had the ability to get them had false papers, so that we could appear as Jews on the one hand or as-- as non-Jews on the other hand. I had two sets of papers with me all the time, a Jewish set of paper here, and the-- and the non-Jewish set of papers here. And we went around, and we violated the-- the hours when we were not supposed to go around, and we delivered papers to people who were in danger, and we try to rescue them. And all that was an intense activity. Which was sort of a-- a-- an outgrowth of our activity in the-- in the Jewish council. Because you see, the-- those of us who were in the Jewish council had these protective papers. And with these protective papers, we could move around. And with the aid of the protective papers, we could even turn into non-Jews, because we went-- there were a bunch of houses in Hungary, in Budapest, which had two entrances. They were sort of through houses. You went in on one street, then you came out on another street. You went in on one street, and you changed from Jew to non-Jew, and you came out on the other street. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were partly manufactured. They were-- part-- partly they were acquired. False papers is a science. You know, false papers are-- there's the lowest grade false paper, which is a manufactured paper, piece of paper. You get the blanks from some official for a few dollars, and you write in the right names and so forth. Problem with papers like that is they won't hold up in the case of an investigation. Because it will say that you were born in such and such place, and you live in such and such place, and they will check with the police. And in Hungary, everybody was registered into an apartment or a place. So they wouldn't hold up. Then you have much higher degrees of paper. I had the papers of a Volksdeutsche. A-- how would you say that in English-- someone who belonged to the German minority in Hungary. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Who, as a result of that, of course, was expected to have the German mother language and who fell on the Eastern Front. And since I spoke German, my mother and I were East German, and I had his papers, everything checked out. When they stopped me, and they check back with the police, is so and so indeed so and so, they-- it-- it would-- it would check out. So you had all degrees of-- of-- all degrees-- excellent papers and bad papers and all the degrees in between.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever in danger of being arrested?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never was in danger of being arrested. I was many times called upon to identify myself. Now, if you call that a-- you know, each of those events are events when your heart is beating in your throat, what will happen now. But I never was caught. I-- I had very good papers. And they were always honored, and I never had a problem. The only time I had a problem was when I hailed a taxi, on one occasion. At that time, taxis-- taxis were hailed on the basis of several people sharing them. There weren't enough taxis in Budapest. And I hailed a taxi. I had to get back home before the deadline of whatever hour it was when I have to be home. And I had my raincoat [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on my arm. The raincoat had a star on it. The jacket in which was had no star. So I had to change from non-Jew to Jew before getting home. I hailed a taxi, and-- and the taxi stopped, and there was an SS captain sitting in it, and I sat next to him. And I suddenly look at my raincoat, and there is the star. And I was-- I froze. I just didn't know what to do. I just literally froze. I said to-- to myself, this is the end. But he was looking straight ahead. I regained my composure and folded my raincoat and got away without harm. But you know, you have many experiences of this. There was the-- the labor. The conscription for the labor battalions, which I escaped. I deserted. I was of course called up, like all the young-- young Jewish people. And there was no-- there was no-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you couldn't get a certificate against that. So I just relied on my-- on my ability to somehow muddle through and didn't leave home, just in case they would come to-- to fetch me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So between the rescue activities, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] visits all over greater Budapest, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] visiting people who were in distress, helping people who were called up for labor service, bringing food to people who couldn't afford it, that was a very busy-- busy time. Where did all the money come from for these activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly from the rich Jewish people. See, by that time, there was no such thing as money coming from foreign countries. The Joint Distribution Committee, which was active up to-- up to the final occupation, it didn't exist anymore. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- the community taxed itself very heavily and not always with success. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Rich Jews gave a lot, and they learned how to give throughout the war, as an increasing number of the population became unemployed. But there was-- never was enough money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, getting back to the transport itself. When the word came down from the Germans to put together this one transport, how was it determined who would go on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The initial idea was-- the-- the deal was really handled by-- by Dr. Kastner personally, and there was no such thing, and what was written about this [INAUDIBLE] there's no such thing as controlling [INAUDIBLE]. There were those who trusted him, and those who didn't trust him. My family and I particularly, I-- I felt very warm about him. I trusted everything he'd do. And if he asked for whatever, he would have gotten it. But we-- we-- we did go by his word. And his word was that 600 Jews would be saved as a sign of good will, on the part of him, the highest authority, [INAUDIBLE] that was never mentioned at the time, the part of the highest authorities, despite the fact that Eichmann personally is disgusted with the idea, because he would love to kill all Jews. But these 600 Jews would go to Palestine with certain conditions. One, that Hitler did not want to antagonize the Arab peoples, and therefore he would not let Jews go directly to Palestine from Hungary via Romania and Turkey and so forth. But what he would do is he would transport the Jews to-- to Spain. And from Spain, if the Jews could arrange via the British and the Zionist authorities to be taken to Palestine, they would be essentially out of the purview of the Germans, and there would be no objection to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then negotiations went on, and then the number was increased to 1,200. From 600 [INAUDIBLE]. And then the selection process started. And I was not in on the selection process, but my father told me. He was deeply in it. How was-- how did--

speaker: interviewee
text: The idea was that 600, the first 600 would be selected on, quote, 'merit,' meaning they would be leaders of the Jewish community who deserved to be saved and their families. And the second 600 would be putting up the money necessary to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to-- that they earn this money for the Germans. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So the spaces were sold. Actually, the number of them was increased to 1,500, and I don't know how. The number that actually showed up, finally, was close to 1,800. I believe the number was 1,784, some such number. Maybe 1,783. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I do not understand how that happened. I know that there were a number of Jews who simply showed up at the ranging station, the ranging railroad station. You know what the ranging railroad station is? They were the main railroad stations in Budapest, there were five or six of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in order to move them around, move the trains around, you had to have so-called ranging stations, where they were moving the trains from one direction into another direction, where they were storing them temporarily before they moved into or out of a-- a-- one of the official train stations. And there was one railroad-- one ranging railroad station, meaning a station where trains were moved around, which was a departure point for the transport. And about, as I say, 1,780 odd people showed up. And I know personally people who simply showed up. And a lot of people wouldn't have, they would have thought that if they show up without identification they would be arrested, but they were not.

speaker: interviewer
text: They got on?

speaker: interviewee
text: They got on. And as a result of that, we were more compressed per car than was originally expected. But there was something like 300 people who were never expected to-- to be on the transport. And there was no control. There was absolutely no control.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who in your family was in the transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father had six brothers. I mean, I'm sorry, my father was one of six brothers. He had five brothers. Each of them had a family of two to three children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The transport left on June 30, and who would participate and who would not participate was uncertain up to the last moment. But tragedy happened in our family on June 14. On June 14, the-- in accordance with the family council, in which I did not participate, but about which I was informed, the heads of the families, meaning my grandparents, the six brothers, and the sister, got together and decided that they will risk-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they will risk a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a-- a venture into Romania, to escape to Romania. And the council determined that two of the six brothers and their families should take advantage of this offer, which was obtained through the SS, through an SS authority. The idea was that the SS truck would come and would pick them up at their home and would take them to Romania, deliver them to Romania, against the payment of certain amounts. And upon return, there would be a letter from the people who, by that time, would be in Romania, and that these people would then be-- the-- the SS people-- would then be-- then be instructed to deliver the letter to the rest of the family, and the rest of the family would make the second payment. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So two of my uncles, each of them with-- with his wife and two children, were collected by the SS and were on their way to Romania. What the SS people didn't-- and were paid-- the SS people were paid partly. What the SS people didn't know was that the code was arranged between the remaining members of the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and the two families who were on the transported, in case things went wrong, the rest of the family should know that things did go wrong. Well, the-- very soon, the-- the message came back, and the code word was that-- was that we are in a concentration camp, we are not in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Romania.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are not in Romania. My father decided to make the payment anyway, which was the right thing to do under the circumstances, and to pursue the matter. And it turned out there was no time to pursue the matter because the two families were on their way to Auschwitz by the time that this-- this happened. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were all together. It was-- it was a very-- a very traumatic experience for the family. The family did not expect this at all. They were absolutely sure that this-- this way out would work. It did not to work. It turns out that in the camp, in the deportation camp in Hungary, where my uncles and aunts and cousins were taken, they met a distant relative. A lady, older lady, who was an aunt of one of my aunts. And anyway, they-- they were transported together to Auschwitz, and they got to the selection point. And they observe what went on from far away. That kids and women were put on the one side, and women without children and healthy young men were put on the other side. And Mrs. Strausser, who was the lady whom we met, turned to my uncles and aunts and said, I will take the children and go on the other side. And so Mrs. Strausser and the children were killed that same day, and the four adults survived for a while. My uncle then died, one of my uncles died, and then the other three survived. Anyway, this-- of course, we didn't know about this detail, but we knew that they were in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there are four families and the-- and my-- my-- Grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents were left. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My-- my father and his family, and at least one of my uncles and his family were incorporated in the transport based on, quote, 'merit,' and the others bought their way in, including my grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where was your mother's family from?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's family was in Hungary. My grandfather died-- died in-- before the war. My grandmother was in-- was in Hungary. She was in Budapest. She actually moved into our-- our flat. But she did not come along. I don't know why. Nor did any of the other-- any of the sisters and the brother of-- of my mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So, that's-- that's what happened. And then we-- we-- we left on the 30th of June. Took us about 10 days to arrive in Bergen-Belsen. And we were shuttled all around, with all kinds of political difficulties that developed with the Hungarian authorities, the Slovakian authorities. The train did not take the-- the path that it was supposed to take, but it finally did arrive in Bergen-Belsen. Was it supposed to go to-- was it meant to go to Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Apparently so, yeah. Not the way it went, but it-- and problems developed between Kastner and Eichmann while we were on the trip, and that caused the-- the transport to be held up for four days in western Hungary. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we didn't know whether we are going to go to Palestine or to Auschwitz, but we finally got to Bergen-Belsen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the negotiations continued. We were not sent to Spain, as originally thought about, or as originally expected. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We arrived on somewhere July, July 9 or 10 in Bergen-Belsen. And in late August the German government decided to make a gesture and to let 318 of us to go to Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to all of you in the meantime? We were in the camp. And we did not work. We were not expected to work. We got the camp food, which at the beginning was tolerable. And then the 318 left for Switzerland and arrived in Switzerland. Were you part of that group?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Nobody in our family was. And then in-- in December, on December 3-- actually, on December 2, we were notified that we are going to Switzerland the next day, on December 3. And we didn't quite believe it, et cetera. But anyway, we did move to Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the time that you were in Bergen-Belsen were you able to stay with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the men were separated from women. But otherwise, we were-- nobody prevented us from staying with each other. We could select whatever bunks we-- we decided to select in the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your belongings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the belongings that we took along amounted to roughly one suitcase per person and not a very big one. And of course, we-- we took that suitcase in the expectation that we would go to Palestine. But that suitcase we-- we kept, and it was with us during our entire stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have much to do with the other inmates there?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean with-- with others than our transport?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, it was strictly forbidden. We were not to mingle. And-- and there were some-- some-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were some very drastic actions taken. The-- if-- if one violated those rules.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody ever violate them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A transport of Hungarian Jewish girls came to Bergen-Belsen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they succeeded in notifying-- some of the girls succeeded in notifying their parents in our-- in our compound that they were in Bergen-Belsen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And against all recommendations that they be quiet, the parents decided to make an application to the camp commander to see their daughter. And they were taken away, and they were interrogated as to how did they know that their daughters were in-- daughter, or daughters, I don't know anymore [INAUDIBLE]-- were in Bergen-Belsen. And they chose to lie and say that they saw this group of girls march by and recognized their daughter, which was a lie. And they were shot. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because the Germans knew that they never marched those girls by our camp, so they knew that there was an underground correspondence.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what became of that transport of girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't know. They were not-- we were not involved with it. But Bergen-Belsen was not too bad while we were there. Actually, there were three stages in Bergen-Belsen, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two of them while we were there. At the beginning, it was just simply not enough food. And the treatment was-- well, it was not good, but it was not murderous. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then came the second stage, when Josef Kramer, the commander of Auschwitz, was moved from Auschwitz to Bergen-Belsen. He became our commander on October 1. And he was not used to the kind of treatment that the Jews got in Bergen-Belsen, so rations were reduced, and things became much harsher. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it-- you know, it didn't last very long, because in December, we-- we lost a lot of weight during those two months. But we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever wonder whether you would survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Wondered all the time. That was the interesting part, but there was no particular reason. You-- you just accept it. You know, you never knew how long. We were wondering when we were told on December 2 that we are going to Switzerland tomorrow morning. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in Bergen-Belsen then during the high holidays.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any acknowledgement of that or commemoration of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. There were services held. And I mean, we had not-- we didn't work, so-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had a bunch of rabbis with us, including the [INAUDIBLE] rabbi, who was with us. So what did you do all day during that period of time, if you weren't working? We read. We talk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] Some, I didn't, but, you know, some people went nuts. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] There was some reading material around. Anybody who wanted to could bring books from home. I had several books of Roman law, and what have you, in some of my [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever a moment where you or anyone in your family gave up hope?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyone in my family, that's hard to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, that you knew about.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, there were some funny events. Because I remember my grandfather, you know, food became very important. And-- and-- there wasn't enough of it. My grandfather was, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] let's see, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was about 70 at the time. And my grandfather and grandmother lived with their daughter, the oldest sister, the only and oldest sister of the six boys. And she was doing the cooking. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember my grandfather telling my aunt that I always thought you were-- you knew how to cook, but really, you didn't know how to cook. The Germans know how to cook. This is magnificent. He said about something. You know, we have some of these odd events.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was it like when the remaining transport members were able to leave? How did that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't know too much about it. I was on the 3rd of December in the morning. The-- the night before, the Germans asked for 30 young men in good health to be available at 5 o'clock in the morning to transport the baggage of the entire camp on trucks to the railroad site, and I was one of those.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to change the tape, and then you'll tell about that.
